title: Lesson 5: The Biblical Storyline
description: The Bible is not a collection of disconnected stories. It is one unified story told in four acts: Creation, Fall, Redemption, Restoration. And Jesus is the hero of every act.

blocks.text: In the last lesson you explored the Bible as a library, 66 books organized in clear sections across two testaments. You now have the map. This lesson takes you on the road trip. Because underneath all of those books, all of those authors, all of those centuries, there is one story being told. And once you see it, you will never read the Bible the same way again.
One Story in Four Acts

Every great story has a beginning, a conflict, a turning point, and an ending. The Bible is no different. From the first verse of Genesis to the last verse of Revelation, the Bible tells one continuous story in four acts: Creation, Fall, Redemption, and Restoration. Every book, every chapter, and every verse fits somewhere inside this framework. When you understand the four acts, you can pick up any passage in the Bible and locate yourself in the bigger picture.
Walk through each act below. Take your time. This is the most important story ever told.

verses.text: In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth.
And God saw everything that he had made, and behold, it was very good.

The story begins with God. Before anything existed, He was there. He spoke the universe into being, from the stars to the seas to the smallest living creature. And at the center of it all, He made human beings in His own image, male and female. He gave them dignity, purpose, and relationship. He walked with them in a garden. There was no shame, no fear, no death. Everything was exactly as it was meant to be. This is what God intended for the world, and it is what He is working to restore.

Notice what God did not base their worth on. Not what they could produce. Not what they could achieve. Not how well they performed. Their dignity was built into them from the moment they were made, simply because they bore the image of God. That is still true of every human being alive today - and it is still true of you. In a culture that constantly measures your value by your output and your success, the Bible says your worth was settled before you ever did a single thing.

So when the woman saw that the tree was good for food, and that it was a delight to the eyes, and that the tree was to be desired to make one wise, she took of its fruit and ate, and she also gave some to her husband who was with her, and he ate. Then the eyes of both were opened, and they knew that they were naked.

The serpent lied. Adam and Eve believed the lie. And in one act of disobedience, everything shattered. Their relationship with God was broken. Their relationship with each other was damaged. The ground itself was cursed. Shame entered the world, and for the first time, they hid from the God who made them. From that moment forward, every human being has been born into a world marked by sin, suffering, and death. We did not just break a rule. We broke the relationship we were made for.
For all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God.
I will put enmity between you and the woman, and between your offspring and her offspring; he shall bruise your head, and you shall bruise his heel.

Even in the moment of judgment, God made a promise: a Rescuer was coming. The rest of the Old Testament is the story of God keeping that promise. He chose Abraham and built a nation through him. He delivered Israel from slavery in Egypt. He gave them His law, His prophets, and His presence. He kept pointing forward, century after century, to the One who would come and crush the head of the serpent.

And then, in the fullness of time, Jesus arrived. God Himself became human, lived the perfect life we could not live, and died the death we deserved. On the cross, He took the full weight of sin and paid the price to bring us back to God. Three days later, He rose from the grave. Death was defeated. The rescue was complete.

It is worth noting that the disciples went on to die for their testimony that Jesus rose from the dead. People will sometimes die for something they sincerely believe, but no one dies for a lie they know they invented - and not one eyewitness ever recanted.

For God so loved the world, that he gave his only Son, that whoever believes in him should not perish but have eternal life. For God did not send his Son into the world to condemn the world, but in order that the world might be saved through him.

Three words are worth pausing on here, because they unlock how the cross actually works. Justice means getting exactly what you deserve. Mercy means not getting the punishment you deserve. Grace means receiving a gift you did not earn and could never deserve. At the cross, all three meet at once. God's justice was fully satisfied - sin received its full penalty. His mercy was fully extended - you did not receive the death you deserved. And His grace was poured out without limit - you received life, forgiveness, and belonging with God as a free gift. That is the gospel in three words: justice, mercy, grace.

And I heard a loud voice from the throne saying, "Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man. He will dwell with them, and they will be his people, and God himself will be with them as their God. He will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain anymore, for the former things have passed away." And he who was seated on the throne said, "Behold, I am making all things new."

The story does not end at the cross. Jesus is coming back. And when He does, everything that was broken in the Fall will be fully and finally restored. No more sin. No more suffering. No more death. No more tears. The garden becomes a city, and God dwells with His people forever. This is where the whole story is heading. Every page of the Bible leans toward this ending. And if you are in Christ, this is your future.

blocks.alt: Golden sunrise over mountain peaks above the clouds

blocks.text: 5.1 - Which of the four acts (Creation, Fall, Redemption, Restoration) speaks to you most right now in your life? Why?
Where Are You in the Story?

Right now, you are living between Act 3 and Act 4. Redemption has been accomplished at the cross, but Restoration has not yet been completed. Jesus has won the victory, but we are still waiting for Him to return and make all things new. That means we live in a world where both the beauty of creation and the brokenness of the fall are real, every single day.

This is why life can feel confusing. There are moments of breathtaking goodness that remind you of the way things were meant to be. And there are moments of deep pain that remind you the world is not yet what it will be. Both are real. And the gospel says that neither one gets the last word. The last word belongs to the One who said, "Behold, I am making all things new."
5.2 - Where do you feel the tension between "already redeemed" and "not yet restored" most in your own life right now?

Living in this tension also means there will be moments of genuine doubt and hard questions. That is not a sign of weak faith. The writers of the Psalms brought their anguish, their confusion, and their unanswered questions directly to God. John the Baptist, from a prison cell, sent messengers to ask Jesus, "Are you the one who is to come, or shall we look for another?" Jesus did not rebuke him for doubting. He answered him. The Bible is not a book that demands you pretend everything is fine. It is a book that invites you to bring the real thing.
Have you ever brought an honest, hard question to God - a doubt you were almost afraid to admit? What happened, or what would it mean to bring it to Him now?
Jesus Is the Center of Every Act

Here is the key that unlocks the whole Bible: Jesus is not just in the New Testament. He is the hero of every act. In Creation, He is the Word through whom all things were made (John 1:3). In the Fall, He is the promised offspring who will crush the serpent's head (Genesis 3:15). In the Old Testament story of Israel, He is the one every prophet, priest, and king was pointing toward. In the Gospels, He is the Rescuer who arrives. In the Letters, He is the risen Lord building His church. In Revelation, He is the King who returns.
Every promise in the Old Testament is a shadow. Jesus is the sun that casts it.

C.S. Lewis pointed out that Jesus only leaves us three options: He was a liar, He was a lunatic, or He is Lord. A man who made the claims Jesus made doesn't give you the comfortable option of calling Him a nice moral teacher - He demands a verdict.
The Road to Emmaus

blocks.alt: A winding path through green countryside

blocks.text: There is a moment in Scripture that captures exactly what this lesson is trying to do. On the day Jesus rose from the dead, two of His followers were walking away from Jerusalem, heartbroken. They had watched Jesus die. They thought the story was over. A stranger joined them on the road and asked why they were so sad.

And he said to them, "O foolish ones, and slow of heart to believe all that the prophets have spoken! Was it not necessary that the Christ should suffer these things and enter into his glory?" And beginning with Moses and all the Prophets, he interpreted to them in all the Scriptures the things concerning himself.

The stranger was Jesus. And He walked them through the entire Old Testament, from Moses through all the Prophets, showing them how every single part of it pointed to Him. When they finally recognized who He was, they said to each other:
"Did not our hearts burn within us while he talked to us on the road, while he opened to us the Scriptures?"

That is what understanding the biblical storyline does. It sets your heart on fire. It takes a book that might have felt confusing or disconnected and turns it into a living, burning, unified story with Jesus at the center. The same Jesus who walked with those two disciples on the road to Emmaus is walking with you through these pages right now.
5.3 - Have you ever had a moment where a passage of Scripture suddenly "came alive" and your heart burned the way those disciples described? What happened?
Seeing the Old Testament with New Eyes

One of the most common mistakes new believers make is to think the Old Testament is a separate, outdated book that does not apply anymore. But Jesus Himself said the opposite. He said the Old Testament was about Him. Moses wrote about Him. The prophets spoke about Him. The sacrificial system pointed to Him. The entire Old Testament is a 39-book arrow pointing forward to the cross.

When Abraham raised the knife over Isaac and God provided a ram instead, that was a picture of Jesus, the Lamb God would provide. When Moses lifted a bronze serpent in the wilderness and anyone who looked at it was healed, that was a picture of Jesus lifted up on the cross. When David wrote about his bones being pierced and his garments divided, he was describing the crucifixion a thousand years before it happened. The Old Testament is not old news. It is the foundation of everything.
5.4 - How does it change the way you think about the Old Testament to know that Jesus said it was about Him? Is there a story or passage from the Old Testament that you now see differently?
A Resource to Go Deeper

If this lesson sparked something in you and you want to see the biblical storyline explained visually, there is an excellent free resource called BibleProject. Their videos walk through every book of the Bible and show how it all connects. You can find them at bibleproject.com or on YouTube. Start with their overview video called "The Story of the Bible" and go from there.

blocks.title: BibleProject - The Story of the Bible
blocks.description: Watch a beautiful visual overview of how the entire Bible fits together as one unified story.

blocks.text: 5.5 - Now that you have seen the four-act story of the Bible, how would you explain it to a friend in your own words? Try it here: what is the Bible about?
Lesson 5 Review

You have walked through the one story that holds the entire Bible together: Creation (God made everything good), Fall (sin broke everything), Redemption (Jesus came to rescue), and Restoration (God will make all things new). Jesus is the hero of every act. And the same story that runs through Scripture is running through your life right now. In the next lesson, you will learn a deeper method for studying any passage of the Bible on your own, building on the foundation you have been laying since lesson 4 of the Starting course.
Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson, and what next step can you take this week?
What have you learned about God and yourself recently in your Daily Devotions?
What is a prayer request for this week?
